food_subgroup: Grilled vegetables with olive oil and herbs

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Italian-style grilled mixed vegetables; closest_parent 'cooked vegetable food product' (FOODON_03301710) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Vegetables and vegetable products — cooked vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (cooked vegetables) is the closest defensible parent category for grilled mixed vegetables with olive oil; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 255620007 'Vegetables (food)' is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for grilled mixed vegetables exists in SNOMED CT.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (e.g. zucchini, bell peppers, eggplant, mushrooms, cherry tomatoes)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of grilled mixed vegetables is approximately 200 g, typically presented as a platter of assorted seasonal vegetables grilled and finished with olive oil and fresh herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed vegetables, cooked, boiled / grilled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite; supplemented with USDA Foundation Foods olive oil data for fat contribution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry covers this exact Italian-style grilled mixed vegetable dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked mixed vegetables (NDB 11998 and related entries) combined with an olive oil drizzle contribution (~5 g olive oil per 100 g dish). The resulting composite reflects a typical restaurant-style grigliata di verdure: assorted grilled vegetables (zucchini, bell pepper, eggplant, mushrooms, tomato) with olive oil, black pepper, oregano, and parsley. Energy and macros are calculated from the composite. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; mixed cooked vegetables analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grigliata di verdure. GI estimated at 40 based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked mixed non-starchy vegetables (GI range 30–50, representative value ~40). Non-starchy vegetables are consistently classified as low-GI foods. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (40 × 6.8 g × 2.0 serving factor) / 100 = 5.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~95 mg per 100 g) and high potassium (~310 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Grigliata di verdure is a low-sodium, potassium-rich dish that supports blood pressure management; individuals with hypertension can include it freely, though restaurant preparations may add extra salt and should be requested without added salt where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and low glycemic load (~5.4 per serving) with high dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI and low GL of this non-starchy grilled vegetable dish make it an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, as it produces a minimal postprandial glucose response; the olive oil content also contributes to improved insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High monounsaturated fat from olive oil, zero cholesterol, and high antioxidant vegetable content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The predominance of monounsaturated fat from extra virgin olive oil, combined with zero cholesterol and a rich supply of antioxidant vitamins (C, E, A) from the mixed vegetables, makes this dish cardioprotective and consistent with Mediterranean dietary guidelines for cardiovascular disease prevention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grigliata di Verdure is a classic Italian grilled vegetable dish consisting of assorted seasonal vegetables — typically zucchini, bell peppers, eggplant, mushrooms, and cherry tomatoes — cooked directly on a grill or griddle until lightly charred and tender. The vegetables are seasoned with black pepper, dried oregano, and fresh parsley, then finished with a generous drizzle of extra virgin olive oil, which adds richness and enhances the natural sweetness of the grilled produce. The dish is a staple of Italian cucina semplice (simple cooking) and is served both as a contorno (side dish) and as a light main course. Texturally, the vegetables offer a pleasant contrast between the slightly crisp exterior and the soft, smoky interior. Nutritionally, grigliata di verdure is low in calories (~72 kcal per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (~6.8 g per 100 g), and provides a meaningful contribution of dietary fibre (~2.2 g per 100 g), vitamin C (~28 mg), vitamin A (~42 mcg RAE), and potassium (~310 mg). The olive oil component contributes heart-healthy monounsaturated fats (~3.1 g per 100 g). The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. Its low glycemic index (~40) and low glycemic load (~5.4 per 200 g serving) make it an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian restaurants such as Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore and is positioned as a premium vegetable side or starter.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Italian grilled vegetables?
Is grilled vegetables good for diabetes?
What vegetables are in grigliata di verdure?
Is Italian grilled vegetables gluten free?
Grilled vegetables with olive oil calories

confidence_reason: No exact entry for grigliata di verdure exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked mixed vegetable composites with an olive oil drizzle adjustment. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous non-starchy cooked vegetable values. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite estimate from approved secondary sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked mixed vegetable entries and olive oil data; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. 2. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked non-starchy mixed vegetables (GI range 30–50); gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for a dish composed of fresh vegetables and extra virgin olive oil; naturally zero by ingredient composition. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish; no animal-source B12 present. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero for a plant-based grilled vegetable dish; mushrooms may contain trace amounts but the value is below reliable detection threshold for a mixed vegetable composite. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite data and are reported as positive numeric values. 8. The input food_group 'Grains and staples' has been corrected to 'Vegetables' as this dish is composed entirely of grilled vegetables. 9. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore; Italian cuisine does not require additional mandatory language coverage beyond English per the Veritas multilingual rules, but Singaporean serving context triggers the Singapore multilingual requirement.
